title: 聞き取れるのに話せない理由とは？

youtube.publish_title: Why You Can Understand Japanese but Can't Speak? | 聞き取れるのに話せない理由とは？

chapters.title: オープニングと誰でも知っているもどかしさ

chapters.title_en: First Reason: Brain Mechanism Difference Between Listening and Speaking
chapters.title: 一つ目の理由：聞くことと話すことの脳の仕組みの違い

chapters.title_en: Second Reason: Psychological Barriers – Fear of Mistakes and Perfectionism
chapters.title: 二つ目の理由：心理的な壁 – 間違いへの恐れと完璧主義

chapters.title_en: Third Reason: Lack of Output Practice and 'Speaking Muscle' Training
chapters.title: 三つ目の理由：アウトプット練習と「話す筋肉」トレーニングの不足

chapters.title_en: Practical Tips: From Understanding to Speaking
chapters.title: 実践的なヒント：理解から話すへ
まとめとチャンネル登録のお願い

in_this_episode_you_will_learn: Why listening comprehension develops faster than speaking ability in language learning
The psychological and neurological reasons behind 'I understand but I can't speak'
How to shift from passive understanding to active speaking with small daily habits
Practical methods like shadowing, self-talk, and output looping
Why it's okay to make mistakes and how to lower your speaking filter
description_intro: Do you understand Japanese podcasts, anime, or conversations but freeze when you try to speak? You're not alone. In this episode of our everyday Japanese podcast, Yui and Akira dive deep into the common frustration of 'I can understand but I can't speak.'

We explore the real reasons — from brain wiring to psychological blocks — and share practical, easy-to-apply tips to help you bridge the gap between listening and speaking. No textbook drills, just natural conversation between two friends who have been there themselves.

Whether you're at N3 or N2 level, this slow, friendly chat will give you both comfort and concrete ideas to start speaking more freely. Let's move from passive understanding to active speaking together.

blocks.purpose: 自己紹介とあいさつ、今日の話題の導入

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: ああ、こんにちは！ユイです。
segments.en: Ah, hello! This is Yui.

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: こんにちは、アキラです。今日もゆっくり話していきましょう。
segments.en: Hello, Akira here. Let's take it slow again today.

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 今日のテーマ、すごく楽しみだったんです。『聞き取れるのに話せない理由』ですよね。
segments.en: I've really been looking forward to today's topic. 'Why you can understand but can't speak,' right?

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: そうそう。これは僕も長い間悩んできたことです。
segments.en: Exactly. That's something I struggled with for a long time too.

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: うん、私も。例えばアニメはだいたい理解できるのに、いざ自分で話そうとすると言葉が出てこない。
segments.en: Yeah, me too. Like, I can understand most of an anime, but when I try to speak myself, the words just don't come out.

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: あるあるですね。特に旅行とか、急に話さなきゃいけない場面で固まっちゃう。
segments.en: That's so relatable. Especially on trips or in situations where you suddenly have to speak, you just freeze.

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: そう！その気持ち、めっちゃわかります。今日はそのギャップ、どうして起きるのか一緒に考えたいです。
segments.en: Yes! I totally get that feeling. Today I want to think together about why that gap happens.

blocks.purpose: 聞き取りと話しのギャップを実感している学習者の共感を得る

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: ねえ、アキラはこの『聞けるけど話せない』状態、どうやって経験した？
segments.en: Hey, Akira, how did you experience this 'can understand but can't speak' state?

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: うーん、僕は大学で日本語を勉強し始めた時、先生の話はほぼ全部わかるのに、自分の意見を言うときは頭が真っ白になってた。
segments.en: Hmm, when I started studying Japanese in college, I understood almost everything the teacher said, but when I had to give my own opinion, my mind went blank.

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: ああ、それすごくわかる！私も同じ。友達が話してるのは聞けるけど、会話に入ろうとするとタイミングを逃すというか…
segments.en: Ah, I know that so well! Same here. I can follow what my friends are saying, but when I try to join the conversation, I miss the timing or something...

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: そうそう。それって実はとても普通のことで、多くの学習者が通る道なんですよ。
segments.en: Exactly. Actually, that's very normal — it's a path most learners go through.

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: えっ、そうなの？じゃあ私だけじゃないんだ。ちょっと安心した。
segments.en: Oh really? So it's not just me. That's a bit of a relief.

blocks.purpose: 一つ目の理由：脳内の処理経路の違い（聞くは受動、話すは能動）を簡単に説明

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: まず大きな理由の一つは、脳の中で『聞く』と『話す』は全然違う作業だってことです。
segments.en: First, one big reason is that in the brain, 'listening' and 'speaking' are completely different tasks.

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: へえ、どう違うの？同じ日本語を使うのに？
segments.en: Oh? How are they different? Even though you're using the same Japanese?

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: 聞くときは、耳から入ってきた音を理解するだけ。これは『受動的』な処理なんです。
segments.en: When you listen, you're just understanding sounds that come in through your ears. That's 'passive' processing.

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: なるほど。受け取るだけなら、まだ簡単ってこと？
segments.en: I see. So if you're just receiving, it's still easier?

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: そう。でも話すときは、自分の頭の中で文を作って、正しい言葉を選んで、口を動かして音を出す。これは『能動的』で、すごくたくさんの作業が同時に必要なんです。
segments.en: Right. But when you speak, you have to build sentences in your head, choose the right words, move your mouth and produce sounds. That's 'active' and requires many tasks at the same time.

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: ああ、そういうことか。聞くだけだと脳の負担が少ないけど、話すと急にフル稼働になるんだ。
segments.en: Ah, so that's it. Listening alone puts less strain on the brain, but speaking suddenly makes it work at full capacity.

blocks.purpose: 具体例で説明：単語を知っていても瞬時に取り出せない理由

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 例えば、『りんご』って単語、聞けばすぐわかる。でも自分で『あの赤い果物…』ってなっちゃうことあるよね。
segments.en: For example, the word 'apple' — if you hear it, you understand immediately. But when you try to say it yourself, you might end up like 'that red fruit...'

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: それ、典型的な『受け身の知識』と『能動の知識』の差です。聞いてわかる語彙はたくさんあっても、話すときにすぐ使える語彙は少ない。
segments.en: That's a classic difference between 'passive knowledge' and 'active knowledge'. Even if you have a lot of vocabulary you understand when listening, the vocabulary you can use immediately when speaking is much smaller.

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: そうかあ。じゃあ、話せるようになるためには、その『受け身の言葉』を『能動の言葉』に変える練習が必要なんだね。
segments.en: I see. So to become able to speak, you need practice to turn that 'passive vocabulary' into 'active vocabulary'.

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: その通りです。これが一つ目の大きな理由です。
segments.en: Exactly. That's the first major reason.

blocks.purpose: 二つ目の理由：心理的な壁（恥ずかしさ、間違いへの恐怖）

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: じゃあ次、心理的な理由ってやつ。これ、めっちゃあると思う。
segments.en: Okay, next, the psychological reasons. I think there are a lot of those.

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: うん。自分が間違えたらどうしよう、とか、変な日本語を話して笑われたらどうしよう、って怖くなっちゃうんですよね。
segments.en: Yeah. You get scared thinking, 'What if I make a mistake?' or 'What if people laugh at my weird Japanese?'

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: ああ、わかる。私、特に初めて日本人と話すとき、頭の中で『完璧な文』を作ろうとして、結局何も言えなかった。
segments.en: Ah, I know that. Especially when I first talked to Japanese people, I tried to make a 'perfect sentence' in my head and ended up saying nothing.

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: それ、すごくよくあるパターンです。でも、実際は日本人同士でも文法間違えたり、言い直したりしますからね。
segments.en: That's a very common pattern. But actually, even Japanese people make grammar mistakes or correct themselves as they speak.

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: ふふ、そう言われるとちょっと気が楽になる。でも、頭ではわかっていても、いざとなると怖いんだよね。
segments.en: Heh, hearing that makes me feel a bit better. But even though I know it in my head, when it comes down to it, I get scared.

blocks.purpose: 完璧主義が話すことを妨げる、フィルターを下げる大切さ

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: そうなんです。『完璧に話さなきゃ』っていうフィルターが強いほど、言葉が出てこなくなります。
segments.en: Right. The stronger the filter of 'I have to speak perfectly', the harder it is for words to come out.

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: うーん、じゃあそのフィルターをわざと下げる練習も必要ってこと？
segments.en: Hmm, so we need to practice deliberately lowering that filter?

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: まさに。『間違えてもいい』『伝わればそれでいい』と思うことが、話すための第一歩です。
segments.en: Exactly. Thinking 'It's okay to make mistakes' or 'As long as it gets across, that's fine' is the first step to speaking.

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 私、最近小さなことから『あ、これ間違ってるかも』って気づいても、そのまま話し続ける練習をしてるんだ。
segments.en: Lately, even when I notice 'oh, this might be wrong', I've been practicing continuing to speak anyway.

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: それはすごくいい練習です。だって、会話はテストじゃないから。
segments.en: That's a really good practice. Because conversation isn't a test.

blocks.purpose: 三つ目の理由：アウトプットの練習不足と『話す筋肉』

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 三つ目の理由は『練習不足』ってこと？でも、聞く練習はたくさんしてるのに。
segments.en: The third reason is 'lack of practice'? But I do a lot of listening practice.

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: そう、聞く練習と話す練習は別物なんです。話すには口や舌の動き、そして瞬時に文を作る『回路』を鍛えないといけない。
segments.en: Right, listening practice and speaking practice are different. To speak, you need to train mouth and tongue movements, and the 'circuit' for making sentences instantly.

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: ああ、まるでスポーツみたいだね。筋トレみたいな。
segments.en: Ah, it's like sports. Like strength training.

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: まさに『話す筋肉』ですね。使わないと衰えるし、毎日ちょっとずつでも動かすことが大事です。
segments.en: Exactly, 'speaking muscles'. If you don't use them, they weaken, and it's important to move them a little bit every day.

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: なるほど。じゃあ、毎日少しでも声に出して練習しないと、聞けるだけのままなんだ。
segments.en: I see. So if I don't practice out loud even a little every day, I'll stay at just being able to understand.

blocks.purpose: 具体例：シャドーイングや音読の効果を簡単に説明

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: そう。例えばシャドーイング。聞こえた音をすぐに真似して口に出す練習です。
segments.en: Right. For example, shadowing — the practice of immediately mimicking the sounds you hear.

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: ああ、あの練習、難しくない？聞き取るだけでも大変なのに、すぐに追いかけて話すなんて。
segments.en: Ah, that practice, isn't it hard? Just understanding is tough enough, but immediately following and speaking?

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: 最初は確かに難しいです。でも、ゆっくりの音声から始めて、だんだん速くしていくと、脳と口の連携が良くなります。
segments.en: It's definitely hard at first. But if you start with slow audio and gradually speed up, the coordination between your brain and mouth improves.

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: ふうん。じゃあ、音読も同じ効果があるの？
segments.en: Hmm. So does reading aloud have the same effect?

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: はい。音読もとてもいいです。自分で文字を見て、それを声に出す。それだけで『話す回路』が鍛えられます。
segments.en: Yes, reading aloud is also very good. Just looking at the text and voicing it — that alone trains your 'speaking circuit'.

blocks.purpose: 具体的な練習法1：独り言を日本語で話す

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: じゃあ、今日からできる具体的なヒントを教えてほしいな。
segments.en: Then I'd like you to give me some concrete tips I can start today.

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: もちろん。まず一番簡単なのは『独り言』です。
segments.en: Of course. The easiest one first is 'talking to yourself'.

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: えっ、独り言？それって変じゃない？
segments.en: Huh? Talking to yourself? Isn't that weird?

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: ふふ、練習中は全然変じゃないですよ。例えば、朝『今日は何を食べようかな』とか、『あ、寒い』とか、ちょっとしたことを日本語で言ってみる。
segments.en: Heh, it's not weird at all during practice. For example, in the morning say 'What should I eat today?' or 'Oh, it's cold', little things in Japanese.

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: ああ、それなら私もできそう。誰も聞いてないし、間違えても気にならないもんね。
segments.en: Ah, I could probably do that. No one's listening, and I don't care if I make mistakes.

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: そうそう。それが大事です。プレッシャーがない状態で、まずは口を動かす習慣をつける。
segments.en: Exactly. That's important. First, build the habit of moving your mouth in a pressure-free state.

blocks.purpose: 練習法2：オウム返しと遅延スピーキング

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 他にはどんな練習があるの？
segments.en: What other kinds of practice are there?

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: 『オウム返し』ですね。ポッドキャストやドラマの短いセリフを聞いて、そのまま繰り返す。
segments.en: There's 'parroting' — listen to a short line from a podcast or drama and repeat it exactly.

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: それってシャドーイングと似てる？
segments.en: Is that similar to shadowing?

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: 似ていますが、オウム返しは少し遅れて、または止めてからやってもいいんです。自分のペースで大丈夫。
segments.en: Similar, but with parroting, you can do it with a slight delay or after pausing. It's fine at your own pace.

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: なるほど。それならハードルが低いかも。あと、『遅延スピーキング』って聞いたことあるんだけど、それも効果あるの？
segments.en: I see. That might be lower hurdle. Also, I've heard of 'delayed speaking' — is that effective too?

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: はい。例えば一文聞いて、頭の中で少しだけ整理してから、自分の言葉で言い直す練習です。これがすごく力になります。
segments.en: Yes. For example, listen to a sentence, organize it a little in your head, then rephrase it in your own words. That becomes very powerful.

blocks.purpose: 練習法3：間違いを恐れない環境を作る、言語交換やAIとの会話

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: でもやっぱり、実際に誰かと話す練習も必要だよね。
segments.en: But still, practicing with an actual person is necessary, right?

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: もちろん。でも、いきなりネイティブと話すのは緊張するから、まずは言語交換アプリで『間違えてもいいですよ』って相手に伝えてしまうのも手です。
segments.en: Of course. But since it's nerve-wracking to speak with a native right away, you can first use a language exchange app and tell your partner 'It's okay if I make mistakes'.

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: ああ、事前に言っておくのはいいアイデアだね。あと、最近はAIと話せるアプリもあるし。
segments.en: Ah, saying that in advance is a good idea. Also, there are apps these days where you can talk to AI.

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: そうですね。AIは絶対に笑わないし、いつでも練習できる。初心者にはとても優しい環境です。
segments.en: Right. AI never laughs, and you can practice anytime. It's a very friendly environment for beginners.

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: じゃあ、そういうところで『話す筋肉』を鍛えてから、実践に行くのがいいのか。
segments.en: So it's good to train your 'speaking muscles' in places like that before going into real practice.

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: まさに。階段を一つずつ登るように。
segments.en: Exactly. Like climbing stairs one step at a time.

blocks.purpose: 本日のトピックをまとめ、リスナーにチャンネル登録と高評価を促す

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 今日の話、すごく勉強になった。聞けるのに話せないのは、脳の仕組みや心理、練習不足が原因で、特別なことじゃないんだね。
segments.en: Today's talk was really educational. That being able to understand but not speak is due to brain mechanisms, psychology, and lack of practice, and it's nothing special.

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: そうです。そして大事なのは、少しずつでも『話す』練習を積み重ねること。完璧を求めすぎないこと。
segments.en: That's right. And what's important is to accumulate speaking practice little by little, and not to seek perfection too much.

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: 私も今日から独り言とオウム返し、やってみるね。みんなも一緒にやってみようよ。
segments.en: I'll start self-talk and parroting from today. Everyone, let's try it together.

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: このポッドキャストが役に立ったと思ったら、ぜひチャンネル登録と高評価をお願いします。
segments.en: If you found this podcast helpful, please subscribe to the channel and give us a like.

segments.speaker: female
segments.text: それでは、また次のエピソードでお会いしましょう。バイバイ！
segments.en: Well then, see you in the next episode. Bye bye!

segments.speaker: male
segments.text: またね！
segments.en: See you!
